content.problemStatement:
There are NNN cars (numbered 111 through NNN) on a circular track with length NNN. For each iii (2≤i≤N2 \le i \le N2≤i≤N), the iii-th of them is at a distance i−1i-1i−1 clockwise from car 111, i.e. car 111 needs to travel a distance i−1i-1i−1 clockwise to reach car iii.

For each valid iii, you can fill the iii-th car's tank with up to fif_ifi​ litres of gasoline, with cost cic_ici​ coins per litre.

After that, you choose some car, sit in this car and start driving in the clockwise direction. To move one unit of distance in this direction, you need to spend 111 litre of gasoline. When you pass another car (even if you'd run out of gasoline exactly at that point), you steal all its gasoline. You cannot move if you do not have any gasoline left.

Your goal is to fill the cars in such a way that you are able to choose an optimal car, travel the clockwise distance NNN and return to the place where you were initially. Under this constraint, you want to pay the least possible number of coins.

Find the smallest number of coins you need to pay. It is guaranteed that there is a way to travel the distance NNN. It can be proved that in such a case, the smallest number of coins is always an integer.
constraints: It is guaranteed that sum of fi over all i is at least N (so a solution always exists).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N – the number of cars (and the length of the circular track). The second line contains N integers f1 … fN – the maximum litres that can be filled in each car. The third line contains N integers c1 … cN – the cost (coins) per litre for each car.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with one integer – the minimum total number of coins needed to be able to travel the whole circle.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In each test case we need N litres total. Buying from the cheapest cars first gives the minimal cost, which matches the shown outputs.

solution.approach: The only requirement for being able to complete the circle is that the total amount of gasoline bought is at least N, because the distances between consecutive cars are all 1. If the total gasoline is >= N, we can start at the car that follows the point where the cumulative surplus is minimal, guaranteeing we never run out. Therefore we only need to buy at least N litres in total, respecting each car's capacity fi, while minimizing cost. Since cost per litre is linear, the optimal strategy is to buy gasoline from the cheapest cars first. Sort the cars by ci ascending, then take from each car as much as possible (up to its capacity) until we have collected N litres. The sum of taken litres multiplied by their costs is the answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case because of sorting.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the (cost, capacity) pairs.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.buffer.read().split()))
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        f = [next(it) for _ in range(N)]
        cars = []
        for i in range(N):
            c = next(it)
            cars.append((c, f[i]))
        cars.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
        need = N
        ans = 0
        for c, cap in cars:
            if need == 0:
                break
            take = cap if cap < need else need
            ans += take * c
            need -= take
        out_lines.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
